coredata.dc:title: Facile synthesis of S, N-co-doped carbon dots for bio-imaging, Fe3+ detection and DFT calculation
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Yan, Jiaying
Zhou, Yuhang
Shen, Jialu
Zhang, Nuonuo
Liu, Xiang
coredata.dc:description: Turning waste into wealth, herein, two highly fluorescent N and S co-doped carbon dots (N, S-CDs-A and N, S-CDs-B) were synthesized by the hydrothermal reaction of contaminant reactive red 2 (RR2) and L-cysteine or L-methionine, respectively. The detailed morphology and structure of N, S-CDs were characterized by XRD, Raman spectrum, FTIR spectra, TEM, HRTEM, AFM and XPS. The maximum fluorescent of N, S-CDs-A and N, S-CDs-B are 565 and 615 nm under different excitation wavelengths with moderate fluorescence intensity of 14.0 % and 6.3 %, respectively. The microstructure models of N, S-CDs-A and N, S-CDs-B, which were induced by FT-IR, XPS and element analysis, had been applied in DFT calculation. The result indicated that the doping of S and N is beneficial to obtain the red-shift of fluorescent spectra. Both N, S-CDs-A and N, S-CDs-B showed highly sensitive and selective to Fe3+. N, S-CDs-A can also detect Al3+ ion with high sensitivity and selectivity. Finally, N, S-CDs-B was successfully applied in cell imaging.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Bio-imaging
Sensor
DFT calculation

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: Financial support from NSFC (Nos. 22108157 and 21805166), the 111 Project of China (No. D20015) and Natural Science Foundation of Hubei Province, China (No. 2022CFB275) is gratefully acknowledged, and the authors thank eceshi (www.eceshi.com) for the AFM analysis and Liu Zhe from Shiyanjia Lab (www.shiyanjia.com) for TEM and XPS test.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Raman spectrum for N, S-CDs-A, (b) Raman spectrum for N, S-CDs-B, (c) FT-IR spectra of N, S-CDs-A and N, S-CDs-B.
TEM and HRTEM of N, S-CDs-A(a, b) and N, S-CDs-B (c, d).
(a) The total, (b) C 1s, (c) N 1s, (d) O 1s and (e) S 2p XPS of N, S-CDs-A and (f) the total, (g) C 1s, (h) N 1s, (i) O 1s and (j) S 2p XPS of N, S-CDs-A of N, S-CDs-B.

(a) UV–vis spectra of RR2, N, S-CDs-A and N, S-CDs-B, (d): N, S-CDs-A (blue-green) and N, S-CDs-B (yellow) solution under 365 nm ultraviolet lamp, fluorescence emission spectra and 3D fluorescence emission spectra of RR2, N, S-CDs-A (b, c) and N, S-CDs-B (e, f). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
The optimize structures of CDs and the corresponding energy gap (eV).

Fluorescent spectra of N, S-CDs-A (a) and N, S-CDs-B (c) (0.1 mg/ mL) aqueous solutions upon addition of different metal ions (Zn2+, Ni2+, Na+, K+, Fe3+, Fe2+, Cu2+, Cr3+, Co2+, Ca2+and Al3+). Fluorescence intensity ratios (F/F0) of N, S-CDs-A (b) and N, S-CDs-B (d) (0.1 mg/ mL) in the presence of different metal ions (400 μM).

Fluorescence images of HepG2 cells incubated with 0.1 mg/mLN, S-CDs-B at 37 °C for 20 min, (b) fluorescence imaging of bright-field image (a); (c) overlay of parts a and b. λEX = 488 nm,. Scale bar = 50 μm.
Synthetic methods for CNDs from Reactive Red 2 and amino acids.

head.ce:title: Facile synthesis of S, N-co-doped carbon dots for bio-imaging, Fe3+ detection and DFT calculation

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Leather Chemistry and Engineering of Ministry of Education, National Engineering Laboratory for Clean Technology of Leather Manufacture, Sichuan University, Chengdu 610065, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Turning waste into wealth, herein, two highly fluorescent N and S co-doped carbon dots (N, S-CDs-A and N, S-CDs-B) were synthesized by the hydrothermal reaction of contaminant reactive red 2 (RR2) and L-cysteine or L-methionine, respectively. The detailed morphology and structure of N, S-CDs were characterized by XRD, Raman spectrum, FTIR spectra, TEM, HRTEM, AFM and XPS. The maximum fluorescent of N, S-CDs-A and N, S-CDs-B are 565 and 615 nm under different excitation wavelengths with moderate fluorescence intensity of 14.0 % and 6.3 %, respectively. The microstructure models of N, S-CDs-A and N, S-CDs-B, which were induced by FT-IR, XPS and element analysis, had been applied in DFT calculation. The result indicated that the doping of S and N is beneficial to obtain the red-shift of fluorescent spectra. Both N, S-CDs-A and N, S-CDs-B showed highly sensitive and selective to Fe3+. N, S-CDs-A can also detect Al3+ ion with high sensitivity and selectivity. Finally, N, S-CDs-B was successfully applied in cell imaging.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The authors do not have permission to share data.

ce:section.ce:para: Currently, chemical dyes from daily life and industrial manufacture have become a troublesome environmental issue because of their chemical stability and toxicity [1,2]. Various techniques have been developed for the removal of chemical dyes, such as membrane separation [3–5], solvent extraction [6,7], adsorption [8,9], flocculation [10,11], bio-degradation [12–14] and chemical degradation [15]. However, those technologies still suffer from low efficiency, secondary pollution and high energy consumption. Turning waste into wealth, these chemical dyes could be used as a raw material for carbon dots (CDs), realizing the green utilization of pollutants. Carbon dots, as a new type of zero-dimensional luminescent nanoparticles, have attracted increasing interest because of their favorable optical properties, high water solubility, low toxicity, low photobleaching, low cost, good biocompatibility, and the possibility of surface modification by various groups [16–18].

The energy gap, electron density, chemical reactivity, optical tunability and fluorescent stability of CDs could be modified by doping with heteroatoms. N, S co-doped CDsare usually used as a component of composite material for photocatalytic degradation [19–21], electrode material [22], catalysis reaction [23,24] and photoelectrical conversion [25]. However, those applications still suffer from unclear mechanisms of N, S-CDs in those composite materials. On the one hand, pure N, S-CDs exhibited a small band gap, resulting in red-shift fluorescence spectra in the visible wavelength region from blue to yellow. On the other hand, the presence of N, S in CDs could improve its possible interaction with drugs, making it suitable for drug detection [26–30]. In 2021, our group first reported the synthesis of carbon dots (CDs) from chemical pollutant reactive red 2 (RR2), via simple hydrothermal treatment. As its excellent blue fluorescent and nice biocompatibility, it had been successfully applied in both in vitro and in vivo systems [31]. However, the luminous mechanism, and the relationship between the nanostructure of CDs and the photophysical properties still remain unknown. In addition, it is still urgent to develop CDs with near-infrared (NIR) emission and high quantum yield.

For this purpose, herein, two highly fluorescent N and S co-doped carbon dots (N, S-CDs-A and N, S-CDs-B) were synthesized by the hydrothermal reaction of contaminant RR2 and L-cysteine or L-methionine, respectively. The structures of N, S-CDs-A and N, S-CDs-B were characterized by X-ray diffraction (XRD), Raman spectra, Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR), transmission electron microscope (TEM), high-resolution TEM (HRTEM), atomic force microscope (AFM), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) and elemental analysis. Then, as-prepared N, S-CDs-A and N, S-CDs-B were applied as the fluorescence probes for detecting Fe3+ and Al3+ ions with highly selectivity and sensitivity. Furthermore, the relationship between microstructure of N, S-CDs and its fluorescent property is discussed by experimental result and DFT calculation. Finally, N, S-CDs-B with yellow fluorescent is selected for cell imaging.
The synthetic methods for CNDs from Reactive Red 2 and amino acids is recorded in Scheme 1 .

Typically, 0.15 g of RR2 and 0.15 g of L-Cysteine were dissolved in deionized water (34 mL) at the flask (50 mL). Then the solution was added into a 50 mL Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 200 °C for 14 h. After cooling naturally, the solution was centrifuged at 10,000 r/min for 30 min to remove the sediments. Next, the solution was filtered by 0.22 μm membrane filter. Finally, the N, S-CDs-A solution was obtained and the yield was calculated to be 79.3 %.

The N, S-CDs-B was synthesized as follows. 0.15 g of RR2 and 0.15 g of L-Methionine were dissolved in deionized water (34 mL) at the flask (50 mL). Then the solution was added into a 50 mL Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 200 °C for 14 h. After cooling naturally, the solution was centrifuged at 10,000 r/min for 30 min to remove the sediments. Next, the solution was filtered by 0.22 μm membrane filter. Finally, the N, S-CDs-A solution was obtained and the yield was calculated to be 90.6 %.

TEM and HRTEM were utilized to observe the CDs on JEOL JEM-2100 F. AFM were obtained on BRUKER Dimension Icon. Raman spectrum was measured on Thermo Scientific DXR. XRD was performed on a Rigaku Ultima IV (Cu Kα radiation, 3 kW). XPS was performed on a Thermo ESCALAB 250XI. FT-IR was acquired on PE Fourier Transform. UV–vis. spectra were obtained on a UV-1900 (Shimadzu, Japan). Fluorescent spectra were measured by using an RF-6000 (Shimadzu, Japan). Element analysis(EA)was conducted on a Germany -Elementar-UNICUBE.
Structures are optimized by the usage of B3LYP-D3 functional theory with 6-31G(d,p) basis set. All the calculations have been performed using Gaussian 16 program package [32].

As displayed in Fig. S1 , both N, S-CDs-A and N, S-CDs-B shown a broad peak at 24°, which is corresponding to the graphitic carbon (111), in XRD spectra (Figure S1, JCPDS card no. 75-0444). In Raman spectrum (Fig. 1 a & 1b), the characteristic peaks of disordered carbon (D-band) and graphite carbon (G-band) on CDs are located at around 1400 cm−1 and 1590 cm−1, the ID/IG value of N, S-CDs-A and N, S-CDs-B is around 0.76, indicating the presence of some defects in the crystal lattices of N, S-CDs-A and N, S-CDs-B [33–36]. Functional groups of N, S-CDs-A and N, S-CDs-B are investigated by FT-IR (Fig. 1c), the peak at 3443 cm−1 is corresponding to the stretching vibration of OH/NH, the peak of 3195 and 3010 cm−1 for N, S-CDs-A resp. N, S-CDs-B is attributed to the stretching vibration of CCH/ArH. The characteristic peaks of 1642, 1404, 1135 and 1044 cm−1 were corresponding to stretching vibration of CO, SO,CO and CN in N, S-CDs-A and N, S-CDs-B, respectively.

TEM and HRTEM have been used to characterize the morphology and the structure of N, S-CDs-A and N, S-CDs-B, those results reflected the good crystalline structure of the N, S-CDs-A and N, S-CDs-B. A broad particle size distribution with an average diameter of around 2.77 nm and 3.15 nm was recorded for N, S-CDs-A and N, S-CDs-B, respectively. The lattice spacing of 0.317 nm and 0.270 nm, which can be attributed tothe (420) and (511) of graphitic carbon (JCPDS 44–0558), are detected in HRTEM image (Fig. 2 ). In Figure S2, AFM image indicates that the height of N, S-CDs-Aand N, S-CDs-B is less than 1.75 resp. 1.00 nm, suggesting that the thickness of N, S-CDs-A and N, S-CDs-B are 5–6 layers and 3–4 layers, respectively [37–41].

Valence states of C, N, O, and S atoms of CDs are investigated by XPS [42–46]. For N, S-CDs-A (Fig. 3 a-3e), typical peaks of 284.82, 285.75 and 288.85 eV are detected in the C 1s XPS, which belongs to CC/CC, CO/CN and OCO, respectively. The XPS of N 1s exhibited two peaks at 398.60 and 400.56 eV, corresponding to the pyrrolic N and pyridinic N, respectively. As shown in Fig. 3d, two characteristic peaks at 531.81 and 532.81 eV are assigned to CO and CO, respectively. Meanwhile, the XPS spectrum of S 2p (Fig. 3e) shows five peaks at 163.70, 164.80, 167.90, 169.10 and 170.40 eV, which are assigned to S2p3/2CSC, S2p3/1CSC, CSO2 C, COSO2 C and CSH, respectively.

For N, S-CDs-B (Fig. 3f-3j), typical peaks at 284.80, 285.78 and 288.48 eV are detected in the XPS of C 1s, which belongs to CC/CC, CO/CN and OCO, respectively.The XPS of N 1s (Fig. 3h) exhibited two peaks at 398.49 and 400.32 eV, corresponding to the pyrrolic N and pyridinic N, respectively. In Fig. 3i, two characteristic peaks at 531.81 and 532.81 eV are assigned to CO and CO, respectively. Meanwhile, the XPS spectrum of S 2p (Fig. 3j) shows four peaks at 162.89, 163.89, 167.49 and 168.49 eV, which are assigned to S2p3/2CSC, S2p3/1CSC, CSO2 C, COSO2 C, respectively.

The chemical constitutions of N, S-CDs-A and N, S-CDs-B were further examined by elemental analysis. The mass content of N, C, S, O atoms in raw material and CDs are concluded in Figure S3 and table S1. Nitrogen content in CDs is much lower than that of raw materials, while oxygen content in N, S-CDs-A is much higher than others. Carbon and sulfur content in CDs are similar to raw materials. Elemental analysis provides basic information for the establishment of the calculation model.

The formation of N, S-CDs-A and N, S-CDs-B can be preliminarily confirmed by UV–vis spectra. The typical absorption peak at ca. 525 nm of RR2 completely disappeared in N, S-CDs-A and N, S-CDs-B (Fig. 4 a). The peaks of ca. 243, 271 and 229 nm represents the π-π* transition of sp2 carbon hybridization, while the peaks of ca. 370 and 334 nm represents the n-π* transition [47–51]. Under the 365 nm UV lamp, N, S-CDs-A exhibits bright blue-green luminescence, while N, S-CDs-B exhibits yellow luminescence (Fig. 4d, inset). Fluorescent spectra of N, S-CDs-A and N, S-CDs-B is dependent on the excitation wavelengths (Fig. 4b & 4e). The emissionpeaks of N, S-CDs-A is from 520 to 669 nm depending on excitation wavelength of 300 to 600 nm, the maximum emissionis 565 nm by using the excitation wavelengths at 460 nm. The emission peaks of N, S-CDs-B is from 605 to 689 nm depending on excitation wavelength from 300 to 600 nm, the maximum emissionis 615 nm by using the excitation wavelengths at 510 nm. Rhodamine B in ethanol (QY ¼ 69.0 %) was used as a standard for measurement of fluorescence quantum yield(QY) for N, S-CDs-A and N, S-CDs-B. The QY of N, S-CDs-A and N, S-CDs-B was calculated to be 14.0 % and 6.3 %, respectively.

Usually, the energy gap between the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) and the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) is related to the absorption and emission spectra. Some theoretical and experimental investigations confirmed that the content of nitrogen is connected to the energy gap, and the effect of graphitic-N is strong than pyrrolic-N without increasing their conjugate size [33,52,53]. In order to relate the blue, blue-green, yellow emission [31] to the microstructure of CDs, the energy gap of CDs with increasing nitrogen and sulfur doping contents without increasing their conjugate size was systematically investigated by density functional theory (DFT) calculations.

Substitution on the outside of the conjugated system is not intimate to the maximum peak in absorption and emission spectra. To have enough places for doping S and N atoms in the conjugated model, a moderately conjugated system model with 54 carbon atoms (54C) is built (Fig. 5 ). The decrease in the energy gap will cause a red-shift of absorption and fluorescence spectra. Herein, the energy gaps of CDs with different N and S amounts are calculated.

In Figure S4, the increase of graphitic-N content can cause the decrease of energy gap without increasing their conjugate size, pyrrolic-N has a similar trend with slightly increasing their conjugate size. This result is consistent with previous studies. In our previous work, the ratio of pyrrolic-N (RN) to graphitic-N (DN) in N, S doping blue fluorescent CDs is 1 to 2 by XPS analysis, considering the sulfur content of raw material, sulfur content of these CDs should be lower than that in this work. In this work, the ratio of pyrrolic-N (RN) and pyridinic-N (DN) is 1:1 and 1:2 for N, S-CDs-A and N, S-CDs-B according to the data of XPS, respectively. Molar ratios and number of N, C, S, O atoms for N, S-CDs-A and N, S-CDs-B are approximately calculated in table S2 based on the mass content data in table S1 and 54C model. Typically, O content in N, S-CDs-A is much higher than that in N, S-CDs-B, O exists in the form of –OH, CO, OSO, SO3H, therein, SO3H function group contains the highest amount of oxygen, and it should be located on the outside of the conjugated system. Hence, S doesn't exist in the conjugated system.

Series 1–3 are calculated in Fig. 5. In series 1–2, the ratio of pyrrolic-N (RN) to graphitic-N (DN) is 1 to 2, nitrogen content in series 2 is higher than series 1, with the smaller energy gap, which is preliminarily confirmed by experiment and theoretical calculation. With the increase of sulfur content, the reduction of the energy gap is calculated in series 1, and red-shift fluorescent was obtained by doping S in CDs, while, the further increase of sulfur content will lead to the increase of energy gap. In series 3, With the increase of sulfur content, the reduction of the energy gap is calculated, however, the further increase of sulfur content has little effect on the energy gap. Conjugated with the optical spectra and calculation result, the microstructure of blue, green, yellow fluorescent of CDs are close to 54C-DN2-RN1 (2.21), 56C-DN2-RN2 (1.25) without S doping in the conjugated system, and 52C-DN4-RN2-S2 (0.85) or 50C-DN4-RN2-S4 (1.08) with S doping in the conjugated system, respectively. The result showed that appropriate sulfur and nitrogen doping in CDs is beneficial for acquiring CDs with near infrared fluorescent emission.

Because of the internal filtration effect (IFE) and aggregation-caused quenching (ACQ) effect, the maximum fluorescent wavelength was observed at 0.1 mg/mL, this concentration is selected for the application of ion detection (Fig. S5-6 ). The selectivity of CDs to various metal ions (Zn2+, Ni2+, Na+, K+, Fe3+, Fe2+, Cu2+, Cr3+, Co2+, Ca2+, Al3+) was explored by fluorescent spectra (Fig. 6). The fluorescence intensity of prepared N, S-CD-A and N, S-CD-B significantly decreased with the addition of Fe3+ ions. On the contrary, the fluorescence intensity of N, S-CDs-A enhanced with the addition of Al3+ ion. Although the fluorescent intensity is slightly enhanced in the fluorescent spectra of N, S-CDs-B, a blue shift of the fluorescent peak is detected. The fluorescence intensity of N, S-CD-A and N, S-CD-B did not change by adding other metal ions, demonstrating the high selectivity of N, S-CD-A and N, S-CD-B to Fe3+ and Al3+ ions. Next, the fluorescent spectra of N, S-CDs-A with various Fe3+ concentrations (0, 10, 20, 40, 80, 160, 240, 320, 400, 800 and 1600 μM, respectively) and Al3+ (0, 1, 2, 4, 8, 20, 40, 80 and 160 μM, respectively) were measured (Figure S7). Fluorescence intensity decreases with the increases of Fe3+ concentrations and the decreases of Al3+ concentrations. In addition, a good linear relationship was acquired with the concentration of Fe3+ in the mixed solution ranging from 0 to 160 μM and the concentration of Al3+ in the mixed solution ranging from 0 to 8 μM, the detection limit of Fe3+ and Al3+ were estimated to be 9.54 μM and 1.41 μM according to the 3ρ rule.

In the same way, the fluorescent spectra of N, S-CDs- B in the presence of various concentrations of Fe3+ (0, 1, 10, 20, 40, 80, 160, 240, 400 and 800 μM, respectively) were investigated, a good linear relationship was obtained with the concentration of Fe3+ in the mixed solution ranging from 0 to 800 μM, and the detection limit was estimated to be 9.85 μM (Figure S8).

Importantly, fluorescence imaging of living cells proved their potential applications in biological studies. After incubating the HepG2 Cell with the N, S-CDs-B (0.1 mg/mL) for 20 min at 37 °C under the excitation of 488 nm laser beam, most of the cells displayed green color under 488 irradiation, which confirms the successful labeling the N, S-CD-B into HepG2 Cells and the remaining emission. (Fig. 7 ).

Two N, S-CDs-A and N, S-CDs-B were prepared from contaminant RR2 and L-Cysteine or L-Methionine by hydrothermal method. The maximum fluorescent of N, S-CDs-A and N, S-CDs-B are 565 and 615 nm under different excitation wavelengths with moderate fluorescence intensity of 14.0 % and 6.3 %, respectively. The microstructure models of N, S-CDs-A and N, S-CDs-B, induced by FT-IR, XPS and element analysis, are applied in DFT calculation, indicating appropriate S, N content is beneficial to obtain the red-shift of fluorescent spectra. Both N, S-CDs-A and N, S-CDs-B showed high sensitive and selective to Fe3+. N, S-CDs-Acan also detect Al3+ ion with high sensitivity and selectivity. Finally, N, S-CDs-B was successfully applied in cell imaging.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Financial support from NSFC (Nos. 22108157 and 21805166), the 111 Project of China (No. D20015) and Natural Science Foundation of Hubei Province, China (No. 2022CFB275) is gratefully acknowledged, and the authors thank eceshi (www.eceshi.com) for the AFM analysis and Liu Zhe from Shiyanjia Lab (www.shiyanjia.com) for TEM and XPS test.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2023.123105.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
